raw_output_payload.content: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print0 | xargs -0 chmod 755; find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l
raw_output_payload.reasoning: Need find .php under /testbed, chmod 755, count. Use find -type f -name '*.php' -exec chmod 755 {} +; then count with wc -l. But need to print number changed. We can use find -print0 | tee? Simpler: find ... -print | xargs chmod 755; then count lines. Use find ... -print | tee /dev/null | wc -l? Actually we need count of files changed. We can use find ... -print0 | xargs -0 chmod 755; then use find ... -print | wc -l? But that would double count. Better: use find ... -print0 | tee /dev/null | wc -l? Wait tee writes to stdout. We can do: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print0 | xargs -0 chmod 755; then count: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l. But that counts after change. That's fine. Combine: files=$(find /testbed -type f -name '*.php'); echo "$files" | xargs -d '\n' chmod 755; echo "$files" | wc -l. But one line command. Use bash: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print0 | xargs -0 chmod 755; find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l. But need single line. Use semicolon. Let's output: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print0 | xargs -0 chmod 755; find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l. That's two commands separated by semicolon. That's acceptable.
